responses.question: can you help me to get statistics for the hazards, like can you count the RAW, WAW, WAR and structural hazards in the pipeline for the code.
responses.response:
You have exactly one true data hazard in this schedule, and no WAW/WAR or independent structural hazards.

Sanity‑check of the pipeline table

The JSON “static_in_order” schedule is internally consistent: every instruction occupies IF→ID→EX→MEM→WB in five consecutive cycles, and only two bubbles are marked:

- ‎⁠400674: fmov w1, s29⁠ has ‎⁠ID[STALL]⁠ in C80

- ‎⁠400678: bl printf⁠ has ‎⁠IF[STALL]⁠ in C80

Everything else is cleanly overlapping, one new IF per cycle.

This is what we expect from an in‑order, single‑issue, 5‑stage model with full structural resources and no forwarding except as implied by the lack of stalls.

RAW / WAR / WAW counts

Now check register uses around the only stall:

- Producer: ‎⁠400668: addv s29, v1.4s⁠

 ▫ Writes ‎⁠s29⁠ in WB at C79.

- Consumer: ‎⁠400674: fmov w1, s29⁠

 ▫ Enters ID at C79, then ‎⁠ID[STALL]⁠ at C80, EX at C81.

Your simulator is stalling ‎⁠fmov⁠ in ID for one cycle so that the value of ‎⁠s29⁠ is available by the time it actually proceeds. That is a classic RAW (Read After Write) hazard on ‎⁠s29⁠.

Because the design is strictly in‑order with a single WB stage and no out‑of‑order completion:

- WAW (Write After Write) cannot occur: any later writer to the same register will always WB after the earlier one, preserving program order without stalls.

- WAR (Write After Read) cannot occur: reads happen in ID, writes in WB, and there is no reordering; a later write cannot overtake an earlier read of the same register.

So, for this whole schedule:

- RAW hazards: 1 (between ‎⁠addv s29⁠ at 400668 and ‎⁠fmov w1, s29⁠ at 400674)

- WAR hazards: 0

- WAW hazards: 0

Structural hazards

In the JSON and table, the only “structural looking” mark is:

- ‎⁠400678: bl printf⁠ has ‎⁠IF[STALL]⁠ at C80.

But that is simply the side‑effect of the same RAW hazard: since ‎⁠fmov⁠ is held in ID at C80, the in‑order, single‑issue front end cannot accept a new IF in that cycle, so IF is stalled. That is back‑pressure from the data hazard, not an independent structural resource conflict (e.g., no two instructions are contending for the same functional unit in EX or MEM).

There are also no stalls around loads/stores or branches beyond that one instance, so:

- Independent structural hazards: 0

- IF[STALL] at 400678 is a consequence of the single RAW hazard, not a separate category.

How the pipeline is executing (in plain language)

Instructions are issued one per cycle and flow through IF→ID→EX→MEM→WB like a textbook 5‑stage pipeline. All those long runs of NEON ‎⁠add⁠, ‎⁠mla⁠, ‎⁠mov⁠ instructions overlap nicely; their source registers are always ready by the time the consumer hits ID, so no stalls are needed.

The only time the pipeline has to pause is when ‎⁠fmov w1, s29⁠ tries to read ‎⁠s29⁠ one cycle too early. The simulator keeps that instruction in ID for one extra cycle (stall), and as a result, the next instruction (‎⁠bl printf⁠) cannot be fetched in that cycle (IF stall). After that, everything resumes normally.

So, hazard statistics for this schedule:

- RAW: 1

- WAR: 0

- WAW: 0

- Structural (true resource conflicts): 0

All stalls in the table are manifestations of that single RAW dependency on ‎⁠s29⁠.
